feedback.text: Myself and many others in my direct environment are worried that digital products, in particular video games, are not required to have any sort of end of life plan implemented in their development. In the case of video games that have some sort of online requirement, this often results in many of these products becoming unusable when support from the publisher ends. Which leads to consumers no longer being able to use the product they paid for. In most cases, consumers will not be made aware ahead of time that their product will at some point become obsolete. In the rare cases that they are, it will hardly, if ever, be stated when that will happen. Hundreds of video games have been made unusable in this way already, and hundreds more are at risk of suffering the same fate. This is unfair to consumers, and is also wasteful from the perspective of digital conservation. I, and many others, are of the opinion that publishers should be required to provide some sort of minimal end of life plan, so that their sold products never become completely unusable. For more in-depth information on this topic, see also the Stop Destroying Video Games EU initiative. An initiative which, at the time of writing, has gathered over 1.438.000 signatures.
